title: Simplest Kids Play Table
summary: A three-board tabletop on a pocket-hole apron and four 2x2 legs — the whole build from one 1x8, one 2x2, and one 1x3, and a fair first project for a pocket-hole jig.
description:
This is about as little table as a table can be: three 1x8 boards joined edge to edge for the top, a simple four-leg apron underneath, done. It's also a genuinely good first pocket-hole project — small enough that a mistake costs a few dollars of lumber, simple enough that the technique, not the design, is the whole lesson.

One real-world detail worth checking before you cut: 1x8 boards don't always measure the nominal 7-1/2" they're supposed to. Actual width varies mill to mill (some run 7-1/4" or 7-3/8"). Measure your actual boards, and if they're narrower than 7-1/2", take the difference, multiply by three (three boards make up the top), and subtract that number from the side apron length so the apron still matches the assembled top.

Pocket holes are what make this project forgiving rather than fussy: even if the three tabletop boards aren't perfectly square to each other on their own, screwing the apron underneath pulls the whole assembly square as you go. That's the actual advantage of building this way over just gluing boards edge to edge and hoping — the joinery corrects small misalignments instead of locking them in.

Leave the finish for last, and leave some slack for it to get used. A tabletop built for kids to color on will get colored on; a few minutes of sanding and a fresh coat handles that better than trying to keep markers away from it in the first place.

tools.note: A great first project for learning one.
Checks the tabletop sits flat once the apron is attached.
Measure your actual 1x8 board width before cutting the side aprons.

materials.name: 1x8, 8 ft
materials.note: The tabletop — measure the actual board width before finalizing the apron length.

cutList.note: If ripping from a 96"-long board, cut at 31-7/8" to account for saw-blade kerf across three cuts.
If your 1x8s measure narrower than 7-1/2" actual width, take the difference, multiply by three, and subtract that from this length.

steps.title: Cut the boards and drill pocket holes
steps.body:
Cut all boards per the cut list. Before you finalize the side aprons, measure your actual 1x8 boards — they don't always measure a true 7-1/2" wide. If yours are narrower, take the difference, multiply by three (three boards make the top), and subtract that number from the side apron length.

Drill pocket holes in all the joining ends — marking an X at each pocket-hole location as you cut keeps you from losing track of which end goes where.

steps.title: Build the tabletop
steps.body: Join the three 1x8 boards edge to edge with pocket holes and glue. Solid wood here means the top can always be sanded and refinished later — a genuinely useful property for a surface that's about to meet crayons and markers.

steps.title: Build the base
steps.body: Drill all remaining pocket holes, then attach the legs to the aprons. Insetting the aprons slightly, or drilling the pocket holes on the outside faces, helps keep the legs from splitting. Check for square as you go.

steps.title: Attach the top to the base
steps.body:
Line the tabletop up with the base and screw them together through the apron's pocket holes. Pocket holes are forgiving here — even if the tabletop isn't perfectly flat on its own, the screws pull it square against the base as they're driven.

Sand and finish, and let a kid loose on it.

images.alt: Simple small wooden kids play table with a three-board top
